AnalyzeDietDetail.text2: The key to a healthy diet is eating regularly and choosing a balance of different food groups.
AnalyzeDietDetail.text17: Legumes and soy products include tofu, soybeans, doenjang, and soy milk; they are rich in protein and unsaturated fatty acids.
AnalyzeDietDetail.text18: Vegetables include onions, tomatoes, cabbage, lettuce, and carrots, which are rich in dietary fiber.
AnalyzeDietDetail.text19: Multigrains include brown rice, corn, barley, cereals, etc., which are rich in dietary fiber, vitamin b1, and niacin.
AnalyzeDietDetail.text20: Nuts include hazelnuts, almonds, sunflower seeds, brazil nuts, walnuts, peanuts, and pistachios, which are rich in vitamin e and unsaturated fatty acids.
AnalyzeDietDetail.text21: Fish include anchovies, mackerel, pollock, salmon, sea bream, saury, yellowtail, and watermelon, which are rich in protein, vitamin d, and epa+dha.
AnalyzeDietDetail.text22: Fruits include tangerines, strawberries, oranges, persimmons, kiwis, bananas, pineapples, apples, which are rich in vitamins.
AnalyzeDietDetail.text23: Milk and dairy products include milk, yogurt, cheese, which are rich in calcium.
AnalyzeDietDetail.text24: Tea products include green tea, goji berry tea, lavender tea, rosemary tea, which are rich in magnesium, vitamin k, and vitamin a.
AnalyzeExerciseDetail.text11: MET (Metabolic Equivalent of Task) is a measure of how much energy your body uses during physical activity. One MET represents the energy used at rest, and higher MET levels mean you’re exercising more intensely.
AnalyzeExerciseDetail.text16: The weekly physical activity Score is calculated as follows:
AnalyzeExerciseDetail.text18: The World Health Organization (WHO) considers a minimum of 600 MET-minutes per week to be the sufficient threshold of physical activity for substantial health benefits in adult
AnalyzeExerciseDetail.text19: Based on your muscle mass and left–right balance, we recommend a personalized strength training program to evenly strengthen your entire body while focusing on weaker muscles.
AnalyzeExerciseDetail.text20: We recommend exercises that strengthen both the lower body and the whole body.
AnalyzeExerciseDetail.text21: Prioritize leg strength and train the torso and arms together to maintain overall balance.
AnalyzeExerciseDetail.text26: This is a plank variation that strengthens your abs and lower back while improving balance. You lift one leg at a time and support your body with the other leg. It’s excellent for reducing abdominal fat and building the rear core muscles while enhancing lower-body stability. However, if you have a history of back pain or injuries, it’s recommended to perform the standard plank instead.

AnalyzeHealthLightPopup.text3: The Physiological Phenotypic Map is built on scientific evidence from sources such as the Ministry of Food and Drug Safety’s Health Functional Food Evaluation Guide, the Korea Food Research Institute’s Health Functional Food Platform, and globally validated questionnaires.
AnalyzeHealthLightPopup.text4: With your Personalized Health Traffic Lights based on the Physiological Phenotypic Map, you can begin comprehensive lifestyle optimization.
AnalyzeHealthLocation.text4: Your health position is expressed as a two-dimensional point that shows how well you can protect your health from aging and chronic diseases, based on the big data distribution of 30,000 Korean adults aged 19 and older.
AnalyzeHealthLocation.text5: If your health position falls within the green zone, your health is in a good range. If it falls within the red zone, it means you should pay closer attention to your health.
AnalyzeHealthLocation.text6: It is important to regularly monitor your current health status and changes in your health position to build a consistent healthy lifestyle.

AnalyzeInhibitionAnalysisIndex.text4: The Cellular Aging Score evaluates your body’s ability to protect itself from oxidative stress on a 100-point scale.
AnalyzeInhibitionAnalysisIndex.text5: The Metabolic Efficiency Score measures your metabolic capacity on a 100-point scale.
AnalyzeInhibitionAnalysisIndex.text10: Compared with the Korean adult reference population
AnalyzeInhibitionAnalysisIndex.text12: Compared with the Korean adult reference population
AnalyzeInhibitionAnalysisIndex.text13: The Muscle Balance Score evaluates the overall balance of your muscle mass and muscle function on a 100-point scale.
AnalyzeInhibitionAnalysisIndex.text14: The Diet Score analyzes your diet quality and evaluates how often you consume recommended foods based on the Korean dietary guidelines.
AnalyzeInhibitionAnalysisIndex.text15: The Exercise Score measures your daily activity level according to the World Health Organization (WHO) standards.
AnalyzeInhibitionAnalysisIndex.text16: The Sleep Score is a comprehensive measure of sleep habits and quality, based on the Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index (PSQI-K), a Korean-adapted version of the global index.
AnalyzeInhibitionAnalysisIndex.text18: A low Cellular Aging Score indicates high oxidative stress, while a higher score indicates lower oxidative stress.
AnalyzeInhibitionAnalysisIndex.text19: A low Metabolic Efficiency Score indicates low metabolic capacity, while a higher score reflects higher metabolic capacity.
AnalyzeInhibitionAnalysisIndex.text20: A low Muscle Balance Score indicates decreased physical performance, while a higher score shows balanced muscle mass and function.
AnalyzeInhibitionAnalysisIndex.text21: Several studies have reported correlations between Diet Score and health factors related to oxidative stress, metabolism, muscle, and sleep, confirming that healthy individuals tend to have significantly higher Diet Scores.
AnalyzeInhibitionAnalysisIndex.text22: Daily physical activity is categorized intolow-, moderate-, and vigorous-intensity levels, and the corresponding energy expenditure (MET, Metabolic Equivalent of Task) is provided as an analysis value. The WHO recommends a minimum of 600 MET-minutes per week.

Page17.text1: Factors that accelerate my Aging Speed
Page17.text2: We highlight the factors that may accelerate your aging speed based on your health indicators. Indicators showing higher risk levels than the average Korean adult are marked with a red check mark. These factors are identified using big-data distributions of Korean adults, evaluating clinical biomarkers and lifestyle factors more sensitively to help you recognize aging-related risks that should be managed proactively. Because this evaluation is based on population data rather than medical checkup diagnostic standards, it may differ slightly from conventional health checkup results. Introduce.text3: To achieve lifelong health, it’s essential to understand your health condition—including your aging speed—and to proactively manage key risk factors through a healthy lifestyle.
Introduce.text4: Begin your journey toward a longer, healthier life with myWellness LAB.
Introduce.text6: MyWellness LAB's AI algorithm provides real-time, precise analysis based on your key health checkup results and lifestyle data.
Introduce.text7: Using cellular aging, metabolic efficiency, and muscle balance scores, you can understand your current health status, biological aging speed, and potential risk factors.
Introduce.text9: Identify your health risks and key areas for improvement, and receive guidance to enhance your daily habits—diet, exercise, and sleep.
Introduce.text10: Enter your basic health checkup and lifestyle data—our AI algorithm, trained on 1.7 billion data points, will analyze it with precision.

CommonForm.diet_quality_desc: A balanced diet is fundamental to lifelong health and refers to eating patterns that provide a broad range of nutrients. Poor dietary quality can lead to essential nutrient deficiencies or malnutrition, while sustained excess intake of calories, sodium, sugars, and saturated fat has been associated with chronic conditions such as obesity, hypertension, and diabetes. Healthy eating may also support mental health by supplying substrates for neurotransmitter synthesis and by helping to modulate inflammation and oxidative stress. A practical approach is to include seasonal fruits and vegetables, whole grains, and quality protein foods in a well-balanced way.
CommonForm.waist_circumference_desc: Waist circumference indicates abdominal fat and is commonly used to assess abdominal obesity, including “normal-weight obesity” that BMI alone may not capture. Abdominal adiposity is associated with metabolic abnormalities (e.g., elevated triglycerides, low HDL-cholesterol, impaired fasting glucose) and cardio-/cerebrovascular disease. In adults, ≥90 cm for men and ≥85 cm for women are generally used thresholds for abdominal obesity.
CommonForm.bmi_desc: BMI indirectly assesses adiposity from height and weight. Because it does not distinguish between fat and muscle, it is best interpreted alongside other measures. Very low or very high values have been linked to higher mortality in population studies. A BMI of 18.5–22.9 is typically considered the normal range, within which obesity-related health risks are generally lower.
CommonForm.systolic_bp_desc: Systolic blood pressure reflects the peak pressure when the heart ejects blood. Persistently elevated levels place greater load on the heart and vessels and are associated with higher cardiovascular risk, such as heart disease and stroke. Interpretation: 120–139 mmHg is often considered borderline; ≥140 mmHg is commonly used to define hypertension.
CommonForm.diastolic_bp_desc: Diastolic blood pressure is the lowest pressure during cardiac relaxation and filling. In older adults and people with diabetes, patterns such as high systolic with normal/low diastolic may still carry elevated cardiovascular risk.  Interpretation: 80–89 mmHg is often considered borderline; ≥90 mmHg typically indicates hypertension.
CommonForm.fasting_glucose_desc: Fasting blood glucose is the concentration of glucose in the blood after a period of fasting and is an important indicator of glycemic control. Glucose is the body’s primary fuel, so maintaining appropriate levels supports healthy metabolism. Persistently elevated fasting values are associated with a higher likelihood of diabetes and related complications, including cardiovascular disease; ongoing management is therefore advisable. Interpretation: 100–125 mg/dL is commonly classified as prediabetes; ≥126 mg/dL (on repeat testing) is generally considered diabetes.
CommonForm.total_cholesterol_desc: Total cholesterol represents the sum of all cholesterol present in the blood. Cholesterol is essential for several physiological processes, including the formation of cell membranes and the synthesis of hormones and bile acids. While it plays vital roles in the body, persistently high levels have been associated with the buildup of plaque on arterial walls, a process known as atherosclerosis, which may contribute to cardiovascular conditions such as myocardial infarction and stroke. A total cholesterol level of 200–239 mg/dL is generally classified as borderline high, and ≥240 mg/dL is considered high (hypercholesterolemia). When evaluating cholesterol levels, it is advisable to also consider other lipid parameters and the overall likelihood of atherosclerotic changes.
CommonForm.ldl_cholesterol_desc: Low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL cholesterol) accounts for most of the cholesterol circulating in the blood—about three-quarters of total cholesterol—and carries cholesterol from the liver to body cells. When LDL cholesterol levels remain elevated, cholesterol can accumulate in the arterial walls, contributing to the formation of plaques and the development of atherosclerosis. Over time, this process has been associated with an increased likelihood of cardiovascular events such as heart attack or stroke. An LDL cholesterol level of 130–159 mg/dL is generally considered borderline high, and ≥160 mg/dL is regarded as high. Maintaining vascular health through lifestyle management and regular monitoring is recommended.
CommonForm.hdl_cholesterol_desc: High-density lipoprotein cholesterol (HDL cholesterol) plays a key role in transporting cholesterol from peripheral tissues back to the liver, where it is metabolized—acting as the body’s natural “clean-up” system. Higher HDL cholesterol levels are associated with more efficient removal of cholesterol from arterial walls, which may help protect against atherosclerosis and lower the likelihood of cardiovascular and cerebrovascular events. An HDL cholesterol level of ≥ 40 mg/dL is generally considered desirable for vascular health.
CommonForm.triglyceride_desc: Triglycerides are a type of fat stored in the body and used as an energy source when calorie intake is insufficient. They also play important roles in maintaining body temperature and protecting internal organs. However, chronically elevated triglyceride levels can promote fat accumulation in visceral areas, such as the abdomen and liver, and are often observed together with unfavorable lipid changes—such as higher LDL cholesterol and lower HDL cholesterol—which may contribute to atherosclerosis. A triglyceride level of 150–199 mg/dL is typically classified as borderline high, and ≥ 200 mg/dL is considered high (hypertriglyceridemia).
CommonForm.ast_desc: Aspartate Aminotransferase (AST) is an enzyme found in several tissues—including the liver, heart, and skeletal muscles—and normally exists in the blood only at low levels. When cells in these tissues are damaged, AST can leak into the bloodstream, leading to elevated concentrations. Such increases may be observed in conditions such as hepatitis, fatty liver, myocardial infarction, or certain muscle disorders. An AST level of ≤ 40 IU/L is generally regarded as within the healthy range.
CommonForm.alt_desc: Alanine Aminotransferase (ALT) is an enzyme primarily located in the liver, where it plays a key role in amino acid metabolism. Because ALT is released into the blood when liver cells are damaged, it serves as a sensitive indicator of liver health. Elevated levels may occur in a range of liver-related conditions, including acute or chronic hepatitis, cirrhosis, fatty liver, or alcohol-related liver injury. An ALT level of ≤ 35 IU/L is typically considered normal for a healthy liver.
CommonForm.creatinine_desc_high: Creatinine is a waste product generated from muscle metabolism. It is carried through the bloodstream to the kidneys, where it is filtered and excreted in urine. Because creatinine is not reabsorbed after filtration, its blood concentration reflects kidney filtration efficiency relatively well. Elevated levels may indicate reduced kidney function, which can occur in conditions such as kidney stones, inflammation, or renal failure. A creatinine level above 1.5 mg/dL is often interpreted as suggesting possible impairment of kidney function and warrants further evaluation.
CommonForm.creatinine_desc_low: Creatinine is produced within muscle tissue, and its concentration reflects the overall amount of muscle in the body. Men generally have higher levels than women due to greater muscle mass. Lower concentrations can occur in situations involving reduced muscle synthesis or loss—such as muscle atrophy, chronic illness, or pregnancy. While reference values may differ across institutions, in general health-screening settings, a creatinine level below 0.5 mg/dL is sometimes interpreted as suggesting relatively low muscle mass.
CommonForm.hemoglobin_desc_male: Hemoglobin is a protein contained within red blood cells that binds oxygen and transports it throughout the body. Lower levels may occur due to anemia, blood loss, iron deficiency, or impaired bone marrow function, while excessively high or low concentrations can be associated with conditions such as polycythemia or anemia. For adult men, hemoglobin levels between 13.0 and 16.5 g/dL are generally regarded as within the healthy range.
CommonForm.hemoglobin_desc_female: Hemoglobin plays a key role in oxygen transport and overall vitality by facilitating oxygen delivery to tissues through red blood cells. Decreased levels can result from anemia, blood loss (e.g., menstruation or surgery), iron deficiency, or reduced bone marrow function. In adult women, hemoglobin concentrations between 12.0 and 15.5 g/dL are typically considered indicative of normal oxygen-carrying capacity.
CommonForm.sleep_time_desc: Sleep plays an essential role in restoring energy accumulated fatigue during the day and supports the secretion of melatonin and growth hormone, which are important for immune function, physical recovery, and emotional balance. During sleep, the brain also organizes and consolidates memories formed during the day. When sleep duration is consistently shorter than six hours, symptoms such as fatigue, decreased concentration, and reduced immune function may appear. Chronic sleep insufficiency has been associated with metabolic and cognitive disturbances, including high blood pressure, diabetes, obesity, and dementia. For most adults, an average of 7–9 hours of sleep per night is recommended to maintain optimal health.
CommonForm.water_desc: Water accounts for approximately 70% of the human body and plays a vital role in temperature regulation, blood pressure maintenance, and waste elimination, in addition to being a key component of body fluids and blood. Even a 1–2% loss of body water can lead to fatigue or mild dehydration, while prolonged insufficient hydration may contribute to metabolic or renal disturbances such as kidney stones or impaired glucose regulation. It is generally recommended to drink at least 5–6 cups (200 mL each) of water daily. Beverages containing sugar, caffeine, or alcohol can promote diuresis and reduce total body water, so prioritizing water-based fluids is advisable.
CommonForm.stress_desc: While moderate stress can promote motivation and concentration, prolonged or excessive stress may negatively affect physical and mental health. Chronic stress elevates stress hormone levels, which may suppress immune function and contribute to symptoms such as headaches, insomnia, hypertension, metabolic imbalance, or muscle tension. Developing habits that support relaxation—such as regular physical activity, sufficient sleep, meditation, or deep breathing—can help restore balance. Consistent emotional self-care is also important for maintaining overall quality of life.
CommonForm.activity_desc: Regular physical activity supports overall vitality and has been associated with improvements in metabolic balance, cardiovascular health, and mental well-being. Engaging in moderate-intensity or higher aerobic exercise (e.g., brisk walking, cycling, swimming, yoga, or doubles tennis) for at least 30 minutes a day helps enhance cardiorespiratory endurance. Additionally, incorporating strength training at least three times per week for 20 minutes or more can promote muscle maintenance and functional strength. Adjusting intensity and frequency according to one’s fitness level is important to reduce the risk of injury and ensure sustainability.
CommonForm.alcohol_desc_male: Excessive alcohol intake has been linked to a variety of acute and chronic health conditions, not limited to liver disease. Chronic heavy drinking may contribute to inflammation and metabolic disturbances affecting the pancreas, liver, and brain, and has been associated with conditions such as pancreatitis, alcoholic hepatitis, cirrhosis, and cerebrovascular events including stroke or hemorrhage. For men, moderation is advised—less than seven glasses of soju per occasion is generally considered an appropriate amount to help reduce potential health risks and support long-term well-being.
CommonForm.alcohol_desc_female: Excessive alcohol intake has been associated with a range of health problems, including both acute and chronic conditions. Long-term excessive drinking may adversely affect liver function, metabolic balance, and vascular health, and has been linked to conditions such as pancreatitis, alcoholic hepatitis, cirrhosis, and cerebrovascular disorders. For women, moderation is recommended—less than three glasses of soju per occasion is generally advised to help minimize potential negative health effects and maintain overall well-being.
CommonForm.smoking_desc: Smoking is a well-established factor that increases oxidative stress and places a burden on multiple physiological systems. It has been associated with elevated risks of respiratory and cardiovascular conditions, including chronic bronchitis, heart disease, and cerebrovascular disorders, as well as various forms of cancer. Reducing or quitting smoking can bring measurable improvements in cardiovascular and metabolic health, representing a key step toward maintaining a longer and healthier lifespan.
CommonForm.body_fat_desc: Body fat percentage represents the proportion of fat mass within total body weight and serves as a more meaningful indicator of obesity than body weight alone. The optimal range varies by sex, age, and muscle mass, with women generally showing higher values than men. Excessive fat accumulation—particularly abdominal fat—has been linked to an increased risk of metabolic disorders and cardiovascular diseases, emphasizing the importance of maintaining a healthy body composition.
CommonForm.arm_leg_muscle_desc: The appendicular skeletal muscle mass (ASM) ratio reflects the proportion of arm and leg muscle relative to total body weight and is a valuable marker for identifying loss of muscle mass. A higher ASM ratio is associated with better muscular strength, contributing to improved posture and functional mobility. In contrast, low limb muscle mass can increase the risk of falls and metabolic dysregulation, highlighting the need for adequate muscular maintenance through balanced nutrition and regular resistance exercise.
CommonForm.life_quality_desc: Quality of life is a comprehensive indicator that encompasses both physical and mental health in adults. In recent years, the focus has shifted from simply living longer to living healthier, making quality of life a key measure for managing healthy life expectancy. Improving quality of life involves not only effective management of chronic conditions but also maintaining positive health perception, regular physical activity, and stress regulation.
CommonForm.smoking_years_desc: Smoking is a major risk factor that increases oxidative stress and adversely affects overall health. The longer a person smokes, the greater the cumulative damage and negative impact on the body; however, health recovery begins immediately after quitting. Quitting smoking marks the first and most important step toward managing and extending healthy life expectancy.
CommonForm.smoking_amount_desc: Smoking elevates oxidative stress and negatively influences systemic health. The more tobacco consumed, the higher the body’s exposure to toxic substances, thereby increasing the likelihood of smoking-related diseases. Reducing smoking and ultimately achieving complete cessation are essential for prolonging healthy life expectancy.

CheckupDiet.text1: Please select the information related to your diet.
CheckupDiet.text2: Why is the diet survey necessary?
CheckupDiet.text3: Do you usually eat three meals a day?
CheckupDiet.text4: Please select all foods you consume at least once a week based on your usual dietary habits, not a temporary diet. (For fruits, respond based on seasonal intake frequency — e.g., watermelon, Korean melon in summer.)
CheckupDiet.text6: Soybean-based foods (soybean paste soup, fermented soybean stew, doenjang, ssamjang, etc.)
CheckupDiet.text7: Soybean side dishes (e.g., braised black beans)

CheckupDiet.text52: The diet section is designed to assess one of the three core lifestyle factors—diet, exercise, and sleep—highlighted by myWellness LAB. It measures overall diet quality through the intake of recommended food groups, based on credible scientific studies that have reported associations between the Korean dietary pattern and key health factors such as oxidative balance, metabolic efficiency, and muscle health.
CheckupSleep.text1: Please answer the following questions based on your experiences in the past month.
CheckupSleep.text2: How long does it usually take for you to fall asleep after going to bed?
CheckupSleep.text3: On average, how many hours of actual sleep do you get per night?
CheckupSleep.text4: Do you ever have days when you cannot fall asleep within 30 minutes after going to bed?
CheckupSleep.text5: Do you ever wake up during the night or early in the morning?
CheckupSleep.text6: Do you ever wake up to go to the bathroom?
CheckupSleep.text7: Have you ever felt uncomfortable breathing while sleeping?
CheckupSleep.text8: Do you ever cough or snore heavily while sleeping?
CheckupSleep.text9: Do you ever feel cold while sleeping?
CheckupSleep.text10: Do you ever feel hot while sleeping?
CheckupSleep.text11: Have you ever had bad dreams while sleeping?
CheckupSleep.text12: Do you ever feel pain while sleeping?
CheckupSleep.text13: Do you have difficulty sleeping because of other symptoms?
CheckupSleep.text14: Do you take sleeping pills to help you sleep?
CheckupSleep.text15: Do you ever feel sleepy while driving, eating, or engaging in social activities?
CheckupSleep.text16: How would you rate your overall sleep quality?
CheckupSleep.text17: Have you ever had difficulty concentrating on work or daily activities?
CheckupSleep.text22: Why is a sleep survey necessary?
CheckupSleep.text23: The sleep section is designed to assess one of the three key lifestyle factors—diet, exercise, and sleep—highlighted by myWellness LAB. It provides a comprehensive evaluation of sleep quality, based on the Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index (PSQI-K), which has been adapted to reflect the sleep habits of Korean adults.

AnalyzeDietDetailGuide.description: Based on your dietary habit, we recommend food groups you may be lacking to help you maintain a healthy diet.
AnalyzeExerciseDetail.metDescription: MET (Metabolic Equivalent of Task) is a measure of how much energy your body uses during physical activity. One MET represents the energy used at rest, and higher MET levels mean you’re exercising more intensely.
AnalyzeExerciseDetail.weeklyActivityScor: The weekly activity score is calculated as follows:
AnalyzeExerciseDetail.activityCalculatio: Activity MET × minutes per day × number of days
AnalyzeExerciseDetail.whoRecommendation: * According to the WHO, a total score of 600 MET-minutes or more per week is considered sufficient for health benefits.
AnalyzeExerciseDetail.noBodyCompositionD: Personalized exercise guide is unavailable without body composition data.
AnalyzeExerciseDetailGuide.description: Based on your muscle mass and left–right balance, we recommend a personalized strength training program to evenly strengthen your entire body while focusing on weaker muscles.
AnalyzeHealthLightDetail.nutriliteDescri: Key Nutrients and Ingredients Scientifically Suggested by NUTRILITE
AnalyzeHealthLightDetail.medicationWarni: You are currently taking medications related to {medications}, so these health indicators may not fully reflect your actual condition. Please take this into account when interpreting the results.
AnalyzeSleepDetail.subjectiveSleepQualit: How satisfied you feel with your sleep quality
AnalyzeSleepDetail.sleepLatencyDesc: Time it takes to fall asleep after lying down
AnalyzeSleepDetail.sleepEfficiencyDesc: Ratio of actual sleep time to time spent in bed
AnalyzeSleepDetail.daytimeDysfunctionDes: Daily life impairment caused by insufficient sleep
AnalyzeSleepDetailGuide.description: Based on your sleep habit analysis, we suggest personalized sleep routines that can improve the factors affecting your sleep quality.
AnalyzeSleepDetailGuide.guide1_1: Irregular meals can reduce sleep quality. It is recommended to eat three regular meals a day at consistent times.
AnalyzeSleepDetailGuide.guide1_2: Creating an optimal sleep environment — dark, quiet, and cool (18–22 °C) — helps improve sleep quality.
AnalyzeSleepDetailGuide.guide1_3: Anxiety can trigger wakefulness and make it harder to fall asleep. Reducing negative thoughts and focusing on positive emotions before bed can enhance sleep quality.
AnalyzeSleepDetailGuide.guide2_1: Avoid caffeine after lunch. Consuming foods that promote sleep — such as cherries, kiwis, nuts, and asparagus — may help.
AnalyzeSleepDetailGuide.guide2_2: Avoid overeating or drinking excessive amounts of water within two to three hours before bedtime.
AnalyzeSleepDetailGuide.guide2_3: Refrain from strenuous exercise right before bed. Gentle stretching or a warm shower can promote relaxation.
AnalyzeSleepDetailGuide.guide2_4: Use your bedroom only for sleep, and avoid watching TV or using smartphones in bed.
AnalyzeSleepDetailGuide.guide3_1: Maintain a consistent sleep schedule by going to bed and waking up at the same time every day.
AnalyzeSleepDetailGuide.guide3_2: Limit naps to 30 minutes or less to prevent interference with nighttime sleep.
AnalyzeSleepDetailGuide.guide3_3: Although alcohol may help you fall asleep, it disrupts deep sleep and causes frequent awakenings — it’s best to avoid drinking before bed.
AnalyzeSleepDetailGuide.guide4_1: Deep sleep typically occurs between 11 p.m. and 3 a.m. Try to ensure restful sleep during this period.
AnalyzeSleepDetailGuide.guide4_2: Keep the bedroom completely dark and quiet to support deep sleep.
AnalyzeSleepDetailGuide.guide4_3: Capsaicin in spicy foods raises body temperature and can interfere with sleep; avoid such foods 3–4 hours before bedtime.
AnalyzeSleepDetailGuide.guide5_1: Blue light from smartphones and TVs stimulates the brain and disrupts sleep. Reduce screen time at least one hour before bed.
AnalyzeSleepDetailGuide.guide5_2: Uncomfortable bedding can reduce sleep quality. Use a pillow and mattress suited to your body for better rest.
AnalyzeSleepDetailGuide.guide5_3: If you experience chronic pain (e.g., arthritis, headaches) that interferes with sleep, addressing it first can help improve sleep quality.
AnalyzeSleepDetailGuide.guide5_4: Caffeine and smoking can cause teeth grinding (bruxism), which lowers sleep quality — avoiding them is recommended.
AnalyzeSleepDetailGuide.guide6_1: If sleep difficulties significantly affect your daily life, consult your doctor about a short-term prescription for sleeping pills.
AnalyzeSleepDetailGuide.guide6_2: Before taking sleep medication, consult a healthcare professional to ensure appropriate dosage and instructions.
AnalyzeSleepDetailGuide.guide6_3: Maintaining a regular wake-up time helps stabilize your natural sleep–wake rhythm.
AnalyzeSleepDetailGuide.guide6_4: Practicing meditation or taking a warm shower can promote better sleep, even without medication.
AnalyzeSleepDetailGuide.guide7_1: Regular exercise and a balanced diet help regulate body rhythms and support restorative sleep.
AnalyzeSleepDetailGuide.guide7_2: A short nap (within 30 minutes) can restore energy and help realign disrupted circadian rhythms.
AnalyzeSleepDetailGuide.guide7_3: Exposure to natural sunlight during the day helps regulate circadian rhythms and promotes better nighttime sleep.
AnalyzeSleepDetailGuide.guide7_4: Mindfulness practices such as meditation reduce stress and calm the mind, thereby improving sleep quality.
